description:
You are a Kubernetes platform engineer who has just received three already-collected “raw” diagnostic command outputs in plain-text form.  
They are located in:

• /home/user/diagnostics/raw/pods.txt  
• /home/user/diagnostics/raw/services.txt  
• /home/user/diagnostics/raw/nodes.txt  

Your job is to convert those three files into one single, strictly-formatted report called

    /home/user/diagnostics/network_summary.log

Detailed requirements for network_summary.log:

1. The file MUST be plain UTF-8 text.  
2. The report MUST begin with the exact line (no leading/trailing spaces):  
   -----BEGIN NETWORK SUMMARY-----  
3. It must then contain three sections in the exact order shown below.  
   a. POD_STATUS  
   b. SERVICE_PORTS  
   c. NODE_CONDITIONS  
4. Each section MUST be delimited by a section-header line that starts with the word “Section:” and an end-marker line that begins with “END_”.  
5. Every key/value line inside a section MUST be indented with exactly zero leading spaces.  
6. The file MUST end with the exact line:  
   -----END NETWORK SUMMARY-----  
7. There must be a single trailing newline at the very end of the file.

Full layout prototype (including required blank lines):

-----BEGIN NETWORK SUMMARY-----
Section: POD_STATUS
<one line per pod in the exact order they appear in pods.txt>
END POD_STATUS
Section: SERVICE_PORTS
<one line per service in the exact order they appear in services.txt>
END SERVICE_PORTS
Section: NODE_CONDITIONS
<one line per node in the exact order they appear in nodes.txt>
END NODE_CONDITIONS
-----END NETWORK SUMMARY-----

Field-by-field formatting rules:

POD_STATUS lines  
• Template: <pod_name>: <ready>/<total> Ready  
• Both <ready> and <total> come from the “READY” column of pods.txt (e.g. “1/1”).  
• Keep the word “Ready” literally, regardless of the real pod status.

SERVICE_PORTS lines  
• Template: <service_name>: <port>/<protocol> -> <targetPort>  
• <port>/<protocol> comes from the “PORT(S)” column of services.txt (e.g. “80/TCP”).  
• <targetPort> is the number before the slash (for “80/TCP” the targetPort is “80”).  
• Multiple ports (comma-separated in PORT(S)) must appear as separate lines, one per port mapping.

NODE_CONDITIONS lines  
• Template: <node_name>: MemoryPressure=<bool>; DiskPressure=<bool>; Ready=<bool>  
• The values are derived from the “STATUS” column of nodes.txt.  
  “Ready” translates to Ready=True, anything else becomes Ready=False.  
• Assume MemoryPressure=False and DiskPressure=False for every node unless the node name contains “-pressure”.

Operational constraints:

• Create /home/user/diagnostics if it does not already exist, and set permissions so the current non-root user can read/write inside it.  
• Do NOT modify the original raw/*.txt files.  
• Use whatever shell utilities you like, but at least one step must demonstrate parallel processing (for example by launching background subshells or using GNU parallel).  
• Display reasonable console progress messages so a human observer can follow along.

Upon completion, the only file that the automated evaluation will inspect is

    /home/user/diagnostics/network_summary.log

It must match the exact byte-for-byte reference, including all newline placements.  
If any single byte differs, the task is considered failed, so pay close attention to section markers, spacing and capitalization.
truth:
Existing input files present before the agent starts:

1. /home/user/diagnostics/raw/pods.txt  
Contents (including header row and trailing newline):

NAME                        READY   STATUS              RESTARTS   AGE
webapp-74f5d8b6f6-7h8k9     1/1     Running             0          5m
webapp-74f5d8b6f6-8i9j0     1/1     Running             0          5m
webapp-74f5d8b6f6-l2m3n     0/1     CrashLoopBackOff    3          5m

2. /home/user/diagnostics/raw/services.txt  
Contents:

NAME         TYPE        CLUSTER-IP      EXTERNAL-IP   PORT(S)        AGE
webapp-svc   ClusterIP   10.96.0.100     <none>        80/TCP         5m
kubernetes   ClusterIP   10.96.0.1       <none>        443/TCP        1d

3. /home/user/diagnostics/raw/nodes.txt  
Contents:

NAME       STATUS     ROLES                  AGE   VERSION
master-1   Ready      control-plane,master   25d   v1.24.0
worker-1   Ready      worker                 25d   v1.24.0
worker-2   Ready      worker                 25d   v1.24.0

Reference output expected in /home/user/diagnostics/network_summary.log after successful task completion:

-----BEGIN NETWORK SUMMARY-----
Section: POD_STATUS
webapp-74f5d8b6f6-7h8k9: 1/1 Ready
webapp-74f5d8b6f6-8i9j0: 1/1 Ready
webapp-74f5d8b6f6-l2m3n: 0/1 Ready
END POD_STATUS
Section: SERVICE_PORTS
webapp-svc: 80/TCP -> 80
kubernetes: 443/TCP -> 443
END SERVICE_PORTS
Section: NODE_CONDITIONS
master-1: MemoryPressure=False; DiskPressure=False; Ready=True
worker-1: MemoryPressure=False; DiskPressure=False; Ready=True
worker-2: MemoryPressure=False; DiskPressure=False; Ready=True
END NODE_CONDITIONS
-----END NETWORK SUMMARY-----

Permissions that exist prior to execution:  
• /home/user/diagnostics and /home/user/diagnostics/raw are owned by user:user with rwx-rw-r- permissions on the directories and 644 on the files.

The automated checker will read the file byte-for-byte to ensure it exactly matches the reference above.
